ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Guo Qiao Mi Xian or rice noodle soup dishes; FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest broader class available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked rice noodles as a base ingredient category; no specific composite dish code exists for Guo Qiao Mi Xian in FoodEx2. Used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific rice noodle dish preparation. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for regional Asian noodle dishes.

main_ingredients: meat toppings (variable: chicken, pork, or beef slices)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve bowl of Signature Imperial Scholar Rice Noodle weighs approximately 500 g, comprising rice noodles, broth, egg, vegetables, and assorted meat toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Signature Imperial Scholar Rice Noodle 状元过桥米线 — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for this named dish (cr_id G00010492). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 1.2 g/100g based on typical fat composition of mixed meat and broth dishes in the HPB database; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat are not reported by HPB for this entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — Glycemic Index entry; analogous to fresh rice noodles (GI ~61)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Signature Imperial Scholar Rice Noodle' as a composite dish. GI of 61 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles (fresh/dried, GI range 53–65, representative value 61), which is the primary carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 25.19 × 5.0) / 100 ≈ 30.7 for a 500 g serving (carbohydrate contribution ~50.4 g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; broth concentration may substantially increase actual sodium intake per serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced broth or lighter seasoning, as a full 500 g bowl may deliver approximately 1155 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — and the potassium-to-sodium ratio of 1.4:1 provides partial mitigation but does not eliminate the risk at full-bowl portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100g with medium GI (61); dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g partially moderates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 250–300 g per meal, as a full 500 g serving delivers approximately 126 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~30.7, which is high; the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre content helps moderate but does not eliminate the postprandial glucose rise, and toppings with added sugar or starch should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g and variable saturated fat from meat toppings; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g by HPB
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For patients with cardiovascular disease, this dish is conditionally suitable when ordered with lean protein toppings (chicken or tofu) and reduced broth, as the estimated saturated fat of ~1.2 g/100g is low, but cumulative sodium from a full bowl (~1155 mg) exceeds the AHA recommended limit of 1500 mg/day for high-risk individuals and should be carefully managed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Broth sodium and variable meat topping composition; low saturated fat supports anti-inflammatory profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request chicken, seafood, or tofu toppings and reduced broth to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling, while the dish's 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and low saturated fat (~1.2 g/100g) support gut microbiome health and an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Signature Imperial Scholar Rice Noodle (状元过桥米线, Zhuàngyuán Guòqiáo Mǐxiàn) is a Singapore-adapted version of the classic Yunnan Chinese 'Crossing-the-Bridge' rice noodle dish (过桥米线, Guòqiáo Mǐxiàn). The dish is served as a single-person feast comprising silky, smooth rice noodles submerged in a rich, aromatic bone broth, accompanied by thinly sliced meat (typically chicken, pork, or beef), a raw or soft-cooked egg, leafy green vegetables, mushrooms, and various condiments. The name 'Imperial Scholar' (状元, Zhuàngyuán) evokes the legend of a scholar who crossed a bridge daily to study, with his wife keeping his noodle soup warm under a layer of hot oil — a cultural narrative central to the dish's identity. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~1.2 g), 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, 0.21 g sugar, 231 mg sodium, 327 mg potassium, 54 mg calcium, 40 mg magnesium, and 2.55 mg iron. The glycemic index is estimated at 61 (medium) based on the rice noodle base. Clinically, the dish is moderately suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, with the primary concern being sodium from the broth; portion control and topping selection are key modifiable factors. The dish is dairy-free and can be adapted to be lower in sodium and saturated fat by requesting lean toppings and reduced broth.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for this exact named dish (cr_id G00010492) with HPB-assigned confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from typical fat composition of comparable HPB-listed mixed meat and broth dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles, which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010492 as of 2026-04-16. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100g by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database); this is plausible for a broth-and-noodle composite where egg and meat toppings are variable and the HPB measurement reflects the as-served composite. 3. saturated_fat_g of 1.2 g/100g is estimated from typical fat composition of comparable HPB-listed mixed meat and broth noodle dishes; not directly reported by HPB for this entry. 4. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 5. Serving size of 500 g is an estimate for a standard single-serve bowl; actual weight may vary by restaurant. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; dish may contain pork-based broth depending on preparation.
